diseases.name: Foot and Mouth Disease (FMD)
diseases.key_symptoms_signs: Symptoms: Excessive salivation, runny nose, wounds/sores on hooves and mouth, lethargy, not feeding, drop in milk production. Signs: Highly contagious, rapid spread through the herd.
diseases.precautions_prevention: Vaccination is key. Strict quarantine of new animals. Do not introduce animals from unknown health status. Report suspected cases immediately as it's a notifiable disease.
diseases.recovery_guidelines: Isolate sick animals. Provide soft feed if mouth sores are painful. Disinfect housing and equipment thoroughly. Expect full recovery in uncomplicated cases, but productivity may be temporarily reduced.

diseases.name: Lumpy Skin Disease (LSD)
diseases.key_symptoms_signs: Symptoms: Firm, raised sores (nodules) all over the hair coat, foul body odor, not feeding, fever. Signs: Very contagious and often fatal.
diseases.precautions_prevention: Vaccination is the most effective prevention. Control insect vectors (flies, mosquitoes) which spread the disease.
diseases.recovery_guidelines: Isolate affected animals immediately. Supportive care with good nutrition and pain relief. Culling severely affected animals may be necessary to prevent suffering and spread.

diseases.name: Contagious Caprine Pleuropneumonia (CCPP)
diseases.key_symptoms_signs: Symptoms: Excessive coughing, labored breathing, nasal discharge, diarrhea, weight loss. Signs: Highly contagious, affects the lungs, often fatal.
diseases.precautions_prevention: A specific vaccine exists for CCPP. Minimize mixing of goats from different herds. Good ventilation in housing is critical.
diseases.recovery_guidelines: Isolate sick goats. Provide a stress-free, dry environment. Antibiotics are most effective in early stages. Recovered animals may have permanent lung damage.

diseases.name: East Coast Fever (ECF)
diseases.key_symptoms_signs: Symptoms: Diarrhea, bloody urine, weakness, swollen joints, excessive salivation, labored breathing, no milk letdown. Signs: Upon slaughter, the meat is yellow and has a foul smell.
diseases.treatment: No specific treatment identified in the source. Veterinary care is essential.
diseases.precautions_prevention: The primary prevention is tick control through regular dipping or spraying, as ticks transmit the disease.
diseases.recovery_guidelines: Prognosis is poor without prompt veterinary intervention. Focus on rigorous tick control to prevent outbreaks. Recovered animals become carriers.

diseases.name: Malignant Catarrhal Fever (MCF)
diseases.key_symptoms_signs: Symptoms: Blindness, rough hair coat, weak joints, dry mouth, wailing sound, restlessness, head shaking, circling, tail may fall off. Signs: No cure, often fatal.
diseases.treatment: No cure. Treatment is supportive to relieve symptoms.
diseases.precautions_prevention: Prevent contact between cattle and wildebeest (or sheep, depending on the virus strain). Do not house cattle near lambing sheep.
diseases.recovery_guidelines: Focus on prevention as the disease is almost always fatal. Affected animals should be euthanized on welfare grounds.

diseases.name: Bovine Ephemeral Fever (Three-Day Sickness)
diseases.key_symptoms_signs: Symptoms: Excessive salivation, shivering, hard stool, no milk letdown, sudden lameness. Signs: Rarely kills the animal directly, but recumbent animals can suffer from other issues.
diseases.treatment: Supportive care: anti-inflammatories to reduce fever and pain. The traditional method of warming the animal (e.g., sun) helps with shivering.
diseases.precautions_prevention: Control insect vectors (mosquitoes, biting midges) which spread the virus. Vaccines are available in some regions.
diseases.recovery_guidelines: Ensure recumbent animals have good footing (soft bedding), are turned regularly, and have access to water and feed. Recovery is usually spontaneous within a few days.

diseases.name: Enterotoxemia (Overeating Disease)
diseases.key_symptoms_signs: Symptoms: Sudden onset, healthy animals found dead. Upon slaughter, blood clots are noticed in the body. Signs: Animals die very quickly without showing prior illness.
diseases.treatment: Often too late for treatment due to rapid death. High doses of antitoxin and antibiotics (penicillin) may help in early stages.
diseases.precautions_prevention: Vaccination is highly effective and crucial, especially for young, fast-growing animals. Avoid sudden access to large amounts of grain or rich feed.
diseases.recovery_guidelines: Focus on prevention. For animals showing signs, isolate and provide intensive nursing care. Increase fiber and reduce concentrate feeds.

diseases.name: Coenurosis (Gid, Sturdy)
diseases.key_symptoms_signs: Symptoms: Circling, head pressing, blindness, emaciation, intense bellowing, ear touching causes pain, diarrhea. Signs: Caused by tapeworm larvae in the brain. No effective treatment.
diseases.treatment: Tetracycline and other measures (ear cutting, sugar water) are generally ineffective.
diseases.precautions_prevention: Break the life cycle: Do not feed dogs (the definitive host) raw sheep or goat heads/brains. Properly dispose of carcasses.
diseases.recovery_guidelines: No recovery guidelines. Prevention is the only solution. Affected animals should be culled to prevent suffering.

diseases.name: Trypanosomiasis (Nagana)
diseases.key_symptoms_signs: Symptoms: Malnourishment (emaciation), dry mouth, hard black dung, swelling of front limbs, no milk letdown. Signs: Blood clots found after death. Highly fatal.
diseases.precautions_prevention: Control the tsetse fly vector. Prevent animals from grazing in high-risk, mountainous areas during fly seasons.
diseases.recovery_guidelines: Prompt treatment is essential. Recovered animals may remain weak and unthrifty. Work with a vet for correct drug dosage.

diseases.name: Anthrax
diseases.key_symptoms_signs: Symptoms: Sudden death (often the first sign). In live animals: high fever, staggering, difficulty breathing, bloody discharge from orifices. Signs: Rapidly fatal septicemia.
diseases.treatment: Do not treat. Report immediately. Antibiotics (penicillin) can work in early stages but are rarely used due to risk.
diseases.precautions_prevention: Vaccination in endemic areas. Do not disturb soil or carcasses in outbreak areas. This is a zoonotic disease and a serious human health risk.
diseases.recovery_guidelines: Do not open a dead animal suspected of anthrax. It causes spores to form. Incinerate or deeply bury the carcass with lime. Report to veterinary authorities.

diseases.name: Actinobacillosis (Wooden Tongue)
diseases.key_symptoms_signs: Symptoms: Difficulty eating, anorexia, excessive salivation. Signs: Soft-tissue swelling, abscesses, and draining tracts on the head, neck, and especially the tongue, which becomes hard and wooden.
diseases.treatment: High doses of antibiotics (sulfonamides, tetracyclines, ampicillin) for a long duration. Sodium iodide is also used.
diseases.precautions_prevention: Prevent oral wounds by removing coarse or sharp materials from feed. Provide soft feed to affected animals.
diseases.recovery_guidelines: Prognosis is fair with prolonged treatment. Isolate affected animals to prevent spread of pus. Culling may be economical for severe cases.

diseases.name: Arcanobacteriosis (Lumpy Jaw)
diseases.key_symptoms_signs: Symptoms: Painful eating, weight loss. Signs: A firm, non-painful, immovable swelling on the jawbone (mandible). Draining tracts may develop.
diseases.treatment: Extended therapy with Penicillin, ampicillin, or amoxicillin. Isoniazid can be used in non-pregnant cattle.
diseases.precautions_prevention: Prevent oral wounds by removing coarse or sharp materials from feed. The prognosis is often poor.
diseases.recovery_guidelines: Culling is often recommended as the infection is deep in the bone and very difficult to cure.

diseases.name: Omphalophlebitis (Navel/Joint Ill)
diseases.key_symptoms_signs: Symptoms: Depression, anorexia. Signs: Painful, swollen navel; purulent discharge from navel; swollen, painful joints (joint ill); may progress to septicemia.
diseases.treatment: Prompt, aggressive antibiotic therapy (broad-spectrum). Cleaning and disinfecting the navel.
diseases.precautions_prevention: At birth, dip the navel cord in a strong 7% tincture of iodine. Ensure calving/lambing areas are clean, dry, and well-bedded.
diseases.recovery_guidelines: Early treatment is critical for survival. Recovered animals may have permanent joint damage and poor growth. Cull severely affected calves.

key_strategies.name: Biosecurity
key_strategies.description: This is your farm's first line of defense. It includes quarantining new animals for 2-4 weeks, controlling visitor access and ensuring they use clean footwear, and cleaning/disinfecting equipment and vehicles between farms.

key_strategies.name: Vaccination
key_strategies.description: Work with your veterinarian to develop a vaccination protocol for diseases like FMD, LSD, CCPP, Enterotoxemia, and Anthrax.

key_strategies.name: Husbandry
key_strategies.description: Good nutrition, clean water, adequate ventilation in housing, and low-stress handling significantly boost animals' natural immunity and resilience.

key_strategies.name: Regular Monitoring
key_strategies.description: Observe your animals daily. Early identification of signs like reduced feed intake, lethargy, or isolation from the herd allows for rapid intervention.

general_guidelines_for_managing_sick_ani.description: Remove sick animals from the healthy herd and place them in a designated sick pen. This pen should be cleaned and disinfected after use and should not be used for healthy animals, especially calving.
Contact your veterinarian for a confirmed diagnosis. Follow their treatment plan precisely, completing the full course of antibiotics even if the animal appears better.
Provide sick animals with easy access to fresh water and high-quality, palatable feed. Soft bedding will help recumbent animals. Reducing stress is a critical part of therapy.
After handling sick animals, always change your clothes and disinfect your boots and any equipment used (e.g., feeding buckets, halters) before returning to the healthy herd.
